2: China is the largest apple producer in the world, with a culti- vation area of 2.27 million hectares in the 2015 growing season and with an export of 40.92 million tons. The rapid development of apple industry played an important role in increasing the income of farmers and improving the diet of Chinese people. However, in China, the increases in apple production is mainly dependent on the high input of agricultural means of production, particularly chemical fertilizers and pesticides. Statistics showed that China's apple cultivation area accounts for 1.7% of the total national arable land, whereas the consumption of chemical fertilizers and

pesticides accounts for 4.7% and 7.7% of the total consumption, respectively (Li et al., 2010). The excessive use of fertilizers and pesticides not only leads to the waste of resources, but also brings water eutrophication, increases greenhouse gas emissions, and further degrades soil quality (Guo et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2012), which, in turn, are all crucially connected to food safety problems, thereby prompting extensive social discussion on how to resolve these issues in China. Organic apples are generally considered to be a healthier and safer food, and are thus preferentially utilized by consumers, especially in China's first-tier cities such as Beijing, Shanghai, and Shenzhen. Therefore, numerous scholars and government officials have called for a switch to organic production and organic food consumption to improve food safety quality and achieve higher economic benefits. Given this, some apple producers in China are engaging in the organic practices. Whether it is conventional or organic production, the current Chinese apple industry has concentrated its efforts on improving apple yield and its associated

economic benefits; however, research studies on the environ- mental performance of apple production in China are limited. So, Understanding how, and to what extent, conventional improve- ments such as organic production practices contribute to relative environmental burdens is a prerequisite to moving towards more sustainable apple production systems. In order to answer these questions, there is a growing body of literature with this focus (e.g., Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Goossens et al., 2017; Hayashi, 2013; He et al., 2016; Hokazono and Hayashi, 2012; Keyes et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2010; Longo et al., 2017; Luo et al., 2011; Meier et al., 2015; Nemecek et al., 2011; Torrellas et al., 2012), and the results were controversial dependent on the different evaluation method, functional units, site-specific, climatic conditions, and so on. Therefore, further research is required at local scales to address unique challenges and opportunities.

Apple production is a complete system that includes its agri- cultural production, transportation, cultivation, and post-harvest links. Therefore, a comprehensive and systematic evaluation of the environmental effects of the entire apple production process is of great significance to the optimization of apple production and reduction of its environmental burden. Life cycle assessment (LCA) is a powerful tool for assessing the environmental impact of a product in all stages in its life cycle, including resource extraction, production of materials, product parts and the product itself; transportation, usage and disposal either through recycling or final discard (Guinee, 2004; Mila i Canals, 2003; Notarnicola et al., 2012; Vazquez-Row et al., 2012). It has been mainly used for: (a) analyzing the origins of environmental impacts, such as global warming, acidification, eutrophication, human toxicity, aquatic toxicity, soil toxicity related to a particular product; (b) comparing factors for improvement for a given product; (c) designing new products; and (d) choosing between a number of comparable products. Since it generally provides the most reliable and com- plete quantification of net environmental impacts, from a macro perspective point of view, and therefore can provide alternatives to decision and policy makers and researchers (Cerutti et al., 2014; Ingrao et al., 2015; Roy et al., 2009).

6: A review carried out by Meier et al. (2015) showed that most of previous studies have reported lower environmental impacts from organic products on a per area and year basis, but higher impacts have been found when evaluating emissions per product unit, mainly due to lower yields of organic farming systems.
With reference to apples, numbers of LCA studies available in scientific literature in the past decade (Table 1), and parts of these LCA studies are summarized below.

Reganold et al. (2001) found that organic and integrated apple production systems were not only better for soil and environment than the conventional but have comparable yields and, for the organic system, higher profits and greater energy efficiency. Jones (2002) showed that transportation was responsible for a consid- erable fraction of the total energy consumption and, in most cases, exceeded the energy consumed in commercial apple cultivation. Mila i Canals et al. (2006), studying the environmental emissions due to the agricultural stages of some commercial apple orchards in New Zealand, identified improvement opportunities from the environmental point of view. Cerutti et al. (2011) examined the environmental impacts of apples in Northern Italy, focusing on the production and retail phases, showing the importance of retailing strategies for the environmental sustainability of apples. Cerutti et al. (2013) investigated three representative ancient apple culti- vars in the Northern Italy and compared the environmental impacts of these cultivars with those of the commercial cultivar 'Golden

Delicious' under three functional unit: t, ha, and $. The study highlighted an important issue in the LCA of food: the impacts of fruit production depend heavily on the FU chosen and the use of different FUs may lead to different results. Alaphilippe et al. (2014) showed that unproductive stages weighted up to21% and 28% of the studied impact categories, in the semi-extensive and intensive or- chard respectively, with little contribution of the nursery stage. Keyes et al. (2015) indicated that fuel use, N and P-fertilizers, and inputs for pest and disease management on both conventional and organic orchards were the drivers of burdens to impact categories. When system boundaries were extended to retail locations, attention is drawn to the electricity used for storage and the role of transportation. Longo et al. (2017) illuminated that despite a lower productivity, preferring organic apples versus conventional apples could help to reduce the environmental impacts for most of the examined impact categories. With a few exceptions, differences lower than 7% occur between the eco-profiles of the two examined products. Furthermore, an interesting and complete review of studies assessing LCA application in fruit production systems can be found in Cerutti et al. (2014).
The main findings of the above studies are summarized in the following list:

<list>Conflicting findings were obtained comparing organic and conventional apples: in some cases organic apples performed better than conventional ones, in other cases they showed worse results. However, the comparison is based on a static situation and not on the evolution of the examined systems; 
Transports to final users and electricity needed for long-term storage were the main cause of the energy impacts of apples. Thus, local sales of fresh apples can be a strategy for reducing the above impacts; 
During the cultivation step, the use of pesticides and fertilizers, and agricultural machines (consumption of diesel) were iden- tified as the main contributors to the impacts.</list>

Meanwhile, to the author's knowledge, with the exception of rare pioneering studies, it can be assumed that mainstream research on the LCA applied to apple production systems began in around 2001. Despite the high quantity of apple produced in Asia, especially in China, most of the LCA applications published inter- nationally focus on case studies in Europe and South America, and no such study focuses on China.

Keyes et al. (2015) indicated that fuel use, N and P-fertilizers, and inputs for pest and disease management on both conventional and organic orchards were the drivers of burdens to impact categories. When system boundaries were extended to retail locations, attention is drawn to the electricity used for storage and the role of transportation. Longo et al. (2017) illuminated that despite a lower productivity, preferring organic apples versus conventional apples could help to reduce the environmental impacts for most of the examined impact categories. With a few exceptions, differences lower than 7% occur between the eco-profiles of the two examined products. Furthermore, an interesting and complete review of studies assessing LCA application in fruit production systems can be found in Cerutti et al. (2014).
The main findings of the above studies are summarized in the following list:

<list>Conflicting findings were obtained comparing organic and conventional apples: in some cases organic apples performed better than conventional ones, in other cases they showed worse results. However, the comparison is based on a static situation and not on the evolution of the examined systems; 
Transports to final users and electricity needed for long-term storage were the main cause of the energy impacts of apples. Thus, local sales of fresh apples can be a strategy for reducing the above impacts; 
During the cultivation step, the use of pesticides and fertilizers, and agricultural machines (consumption of diesel) were iden- tified as the main contributors to the impacts.</list>

Meanwhile, to the author's knowledge, with the exception of rare pioneering studies, it can be assumed that mainstream research on the LCA applied to apple production systems began in around 2001. Despite the high quantity of apple produced in Asia, especially in China, most of the LCA applications published inter- nationally focus on case studies in Europe and South America, and no such study focuses on China.

14: <header>1.3. The goal of this LCA study </header>

LCA can, however, be hindered by the lack of representative data inventories, a factor that has been recognized as one of the main drawbacks affecting the reliability of LCA, especially in the agri- cultural sector (Niero et al., 2015). Accounting for different site- specific and climatic conditions can have a significant effect on the LCA results (Martínez-Blanco et al., 2013). Furthermore, the plant production systems are very diverse due to the numerous management decisions and variety of agricultural practices (Fedele et al., 2014). So, using general data for one specific case study is common practice in LCA because of lack of site-specific data, but can generate biased environmental impact results. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate which input factors influence the envi- ronmental performance the most and should be adapted to regional and temporal characteristics.

There are some differences between the Bohai Bay and the Loess Plateau regions in terms of climatic conditions and management, and the difference in yield is also significant. At the same time, China's Ministry of Agriculture proposed the changing of the development of apple-producing areas from east (Bohai Bay region) to west (Loess Plateau region), which means, the area of apple cultivation in the Loess Plateau region will expand, while the Bohai Bay region will gradually decrease.

To this context, the impetus for this research was: 1) further contribution to the limited number of LCA studies of apples; 2) the comparison between organic and conventional apples produced in the same and different climatic conditions; 3) to identify oppor- tunities to improve the environmental performance of regional conventional and organic apple production systems to better

position the sectors in the face of inevitable increased environ- mental scrutiny; 4) Provide a reference for the government to develop the development policy of apple fruit. More broadly, it is hoped that substantive and methodological insights from the work will benefit the broader fruit system and LCA practice communities in China.

16: Four apple production systems were identified in two main apple production regions, one in Yantai City of Shandong Province in the Bohai Bay area, and the other in Yan'an City of Shaanxi Province in the Loess Plateau area (Fig. 1). These four apple pro- duction systems are encoded using the combination of regions (e.g., SD for Shandong Province and SX for Shaanxi Province) and farming system (e.g., CON for conventional system and ORG for organic system).

Yantai City of Shandong Province has a typical continental monsoon climate featuring warm and semi-humid with rainfalls in summer and dry cold days in winter. The average annual temper- ature is 11.8 total hours of sunshine is 2698.4 h and precipita- tion is 651.9 mm. This region is the traditional and advantageous area for apple production in China, the apple planting area is 18.67 X 104 ha, and its output was 4.65 million tons in 2014. Yan'an City in Shaanxi Province is characterized by semi-arid conditions and high soil erosion. The average annual temperature, total hours of sunshine, and precipitation is 9.2° 2448.61 and 486.6 mm, respectively. This region is considered to be the most suitable area for apple production due to its favourable natural weather condi- tions, the apple planting area and output are 23.3 X 104 ha and 2.82 million tons in 2014, respectively. These two areas well represent the characteristics of apple production in the Bohai Bay and Loess Plateau apple production area.

18: According to the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a; b), the LCA method consists of four phases: goal and scope definition, inventory analysis, impact assessment, and interpretation. The
Fig. 1. Locations of the conventional/organic apple production system in Shandong and Shaanxi Provinces in China.
main decisions made during these four phases are briefly described in the following sections.
19: The objectives of this study were: 1) To determine environ- mental impacts (including demand for non-renewable energy re- sources, land resource depletion, water resource depletion, global warming, acidification, eutrophication, human toxicity, aquatic toxicity, and soil toxicity) in these two production chains and to analyze the major causes for the differences of these two variables between the production systems; 2) To distinguish the processes that differ between the production systems and to discuss options for reducing environmental impacts.
20: The study focuses on apple production chains (apple cultivation and distribution but excluding consumption), that is, the system boundaries included in the life cycle stages are from the cradle (mineral and fossil fuel extraction) to the point-of-sale. In this research, the life cycle stages was divided into three sub-stages: agricultural materials input stage (included exploration and pro- cessing of fertilizer, diesel, pesticide, paper bags, mulching film), orchard management stage (included soil management, fertiliza- tion, irrigation, film mulching, bagging, pruning, and pest control; excluding farm machinery production and infrastructure con- struction), and off-farm stage (included storage inputs, and trans- port to various retail locations; excluding retailing, home consumption and waste management) (Fig. 2). Based on the modelling of consequential LCA, the process of package is not considered as part of the system because it is not affected by the choice of chains.
According to ISO standards, the functional unit (FU) is defined as a quantified performance of a product system to be used as a reference unit in an LCA (ISO, 2006a). In this study, in order to

compare the results obtained according to yield a variations over the organic and conventional production systems, a mass-based functional unit was chosen, so the FU for this study is defined as the production of one tonne of fresh apples.

22: In this step, the primary data related to the foreground system were mainly gathered in face-to-face interview and the field in- vestigations and are listed in Table 2 for each system. Data on the SD-CON, SD-ORG, SX-CON, and SX-ORG apple production systems were collected by conducted a questionnaire-based interview of 43, 10, 54, and 12 producers or production managers, respectively, and the data were obtained during the course of the 2014/15 season. The data on fruit sales and storage were gathered from relevant experts, local agricultural bureaus, and fruit sellers. All orchards included were predominated by 'Fuji' apple trees, and had been in the productive phase for a minimum of 12 years and a maximum of 19 years. Through market research, we postulated that the average transportation distance of fertilizer, manure, paper bag, plastic film, and pesticide from the place of production to the field were 80, 60, 30, 20, and 40km, respectively. After harvest, fruit grading was based on artificial classification. Therefore, the environmental ef- fects of the machinery used in the refrigeration process were not taken into account in this study. The energy consumption in the fruit processing industry (such as juice and apple vinegar produc- tion) was also not taken into account in this study. The apples in SD- CON and SX-CON systems were sold across the country and mainly in the surrounding cities, and thus in this study, we assumed that for conventional apples, the average transport distance from the planting field to retailing market was about 560 km. The organic apples are mainly sold to Beijing, Shanghai, Shenzhen, and other first-tier cities, and the average transport distance is about 850 km. The general means of transport involved minivans, with an average diesel consumption of 0.1 L/t.km. There were also differences in the

conditions of storage between SX and SD Provinces. About 80% of the apples in SX Province were subjected to traditional cold storage, whereas the controlled atmosphere (CA) storage was mainly used in SD Province. The four production systems differed in the amounts of fertilizer, organic manure, pesticide, paper bags, mulching film, and in the proportion of storage and storage time (see Table 2 for details).

Field level greenhouse gas emissions associated with fertilizers applied in the different apple production systems mainly depended on the types of fertilizers: i.e., organic manure or synthetic fertil- izers. In this study, greenhouse gas emissions arising from using the organic manure were calculated according to Ji et al. (2012) and Shen et al. (2015). Nitrogen leaching and phosphorus loss were calculated as 18% and 0.2% of inputs from organic nitrogen and organic phosphorus (Lu, 2009; Lu et al., 2014). For synthetic

fertilizers, the respective ammonia volatilizations in SD Province and SX Province were 4.46% and 8.74% of the nitrogen input (Ge et al., 2010, 2014), nitrate leaching was estimated as 23.26% of the nitrogen input in SD Province (Ge et al., 2011) and 17.21% in SX Province (Chen et al., 2014; Fan et al., 2013). Direct N20 emissions emerged during the application of inorganic nitrogen and organic fertilizer, accounting for 2.31% of the N released as N20 in SD Province (Ge et al., 2015) and 3.01% in SX Province (Pang et al., 2009). Induced emissions from ammonia and nitrate losses were considered besides the direct emissions. The respective factors were 1% for ammonia-N and 2.5% for nitrate-N (Nemecek et al., 2008). NOx-N was calculated as 10% of the N20 emissions (Brentrup et al., 2004). Phosphorus (P) loss to water occurred through three paths, namely, run-off (as phosphate), erosion (as phosphorus) to rivers, and leaching to ground water (as
phosphate). In the present study, P loss was calculated as 0.2% of the inputs of both inorganic and organic P sources (Wang et al., 2007).

Biogenic carbon dioxide (CO2) fixation was taken into account and included in the LCA. During apple growth inorganic carbon, in the form of CO2, is absorbed from the atmosphere and converted through photosynthesis to organic compounds that are incorpo- rated into the plant mass. According to Wu et al. (2012) and Bai et al. (2015), 46.58 of CO2 are fixated to 119.82 g of fresh weight apple tree.

In the conventional apple production system, pesticide losses to air were determined using a standard residue rate of 10% per unit weight of pesticides, 1% to freshwater, and 43% to soil (Wang et al., 2012). In organic apple production, different agriculture measures were used to control pests, with some physical measures such as color plate traps and sex pheromones. Sometimes farmers also use small amounts of bio-pesticides instead of chemical pesticides. The toxicity of bio-pesticides was not considered in our analysis as farmers usually apply a small amount of bio-pesticides that easily decomposes in the environment with little toxicity for human beings.

Heavy metals (Cd, Pb, As, Cu, and Zn) losses were considered in terms of input-output balance. Heavy metal inputs were generally in the form of organic manure, fertilizers, and pesticides. The out- puts were in the form of harvested fruits, erosion, and leaching. The input-output balance of heavy metals in the apple production system was estimated according to Jiang et al. (2007) and Liang. (2009).

Data connected with the background system (production of fertilizers and pesticides, fuel, transportation, etc.) were taken from the ecoinvent 2.2 database (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2012) and additional peer-reviewed LCA data when necessary. Table S1 lists the data sources of the background information.

Among orchard management stage, allocation rules were used when the same machinery was shared by two different field pro- cesses. For example, the same equipment was used to plough in the compost (field process: fertilization) and the leaf litter (field pro- cess: sanitation). To calculate the impacts, 50% of the 'environ- mental cost' of the machinery use was associated with the fertilization activity and 50% with the sanitation practice, which is part of plant protection.

With regard to the environmental impacts associated with the main processes of production of one ton of apple fruit because there were no co-products, no allocation was done in accordance with the ISO standards: 100% of the environmental impacts corre- sponded to one ton of fruit in the analyzed apple orchard. Furthermore, grade specific (i.e., direct consumption; processing) allocation of apples was not conducted in this analysis, as has been undertaken in some past apple LCA research (e.g., Mila i Canals et al., 2006; Sim et al., 2007). Although co-production of grade specific apples occurs, both those intended for direct consumption and those for processing are sent to storage facilities before reaching their final destinations, thereby using inputs involved in the storage process. Separate partitioning of apple grades was also unnecessary due to the fact that an integrated average of apples stored throughout the year was used to determine the amount of materials used per tonne of apples, following the project's objec- tives to understand the environmental impacts of typical com- mercial (conventional and organic) apple production in China, rather than those with superior or inferior economic value. More- over, grade specific data were unavailable for this study.

conditions of storage between SX and SD Provinces. About 80% of the apples in SX Province were subjected to traditional cold storage, whereas the controlled atmosphere (CA) storage was mainly used in SD Province. The four production systems differed in the amounts of fertilizer, organic manure, pesticide, paper bags, mulching film, and in the proportion of storage and storage time (see Table 2 for details).

Field level greenhouse gas emissions associated with fertilizers applied in the different apple production systems mainly depended on the types of fertilizers: i.e., organic manure or synthetic fertil- izers. In this study, greenhouse gas emissions arising from using the organic manure were calculated according to Ji et al. (2012) and Shen et al. (2015). Nitrogen leaching and phosphorus loss were calculated as 18% and 0.2% of inputs from organic nitrogen and organic phosphorus (Lu, 2009; Lu et al., 2014). For synthetic

fertilizers, the respective ammonia volatilizations in SD Province and SX Province were 4.46% and 8.74% of the nitrogen input (Ge et al., 2010, 2014), nitrate leaching was estimated as 23.26% of the nitrogen input in SD Province (Ge et al., 2011) and 17.21% in SX Province (Chen et al., 2014; Fan et al., 2013). Direct N20 emissions emerged during the application of inorganic nitrogen and organic fertilizer, accounting for 2.31% of the N released as N20 in SD Province (Ge et al., 2015) and 3.01% in SX Province (Pang et al., 2009). Induced emissions from ammonia and nitrate losses were considered besides the direct emissions. The respective factors were 1% for ammonia-N and 2.5% for nitrate-N (Nemecek et al., 2008). NOx-N was calculated as 10% of the N20 emissions (Brentrup et al., 2004). Phosphorus (P) loss to water occurred through three paths, namely, run-off (as phosphate), erosion (as phosphorus) to rivers, and leaching to ground water (as
phosphate). In the present study, P loss was calculated as 0.2% of the inputs of both inorganic and organic P sources (Wang et al., 2007).

Biogenic carbon dioxide (CO2) fixation was taken into account and included in the LCA. During apple growth inorganic carbon, in the form of CO2, is absorbed from the atmosphere and converted through photosynthesis to organic compounds that are incorpo- rated into the plant mass. According to Wu et al. (2012) and Bai et al. (2015), 46.58 of CO2 are fixated to 119.82 g of fresh weight apple tree.

In the conventional apple production system, pesticide losses to air were determined using a standard residue rate of 10% per unit weight of pesticides, 1% to freshwater, and 43% to soil (Wang et al., 2012). In organic apple production, different agriculture measures were used to control pests, with some physical measures such as color plate traps and sex pheromones. Sometimes farmers also use small amounts of bio-pesticides instead of chemical pesticides. The toxicity of bio-pesticides was not considered in our analysis as farmers usually apply a small amount of bio-pesticides that easily decomposes in the environment with little toxicity for human beings.

Heavy metals (Cd, Pb, As, Cu, and Zn) losses were considered in terms of input-output balance. Heavy metal inputs were generally in the form of organic manure, fertilizers, and pesticides. The out- puts were in the form of harvested fruits, erosion, and leaching. The input-output balance of heavy metals in the apple production system was estimated according to Jiang et al. (2007) and Liang. (2009).

Data connected with the background system (production of fertilizers and pesticides, fuel, transportation, etc.) were taken from the ecoinvent 2.2 database (Swiss Centre for Life Cycle Inventories, 2012) and additional peer-reviewed LCA data when necessary. Table S1 lists the data sources of the background information.

Among orchard management stage, allocation rules were used when the same machinery was shared by two different field pro- cesses. For example, the same equipment was used to plough in the compost (field process: fertilization) and the leaf litter (field pro- cess: sanitation). To calculate the impacts, 50% of the 'environ- mental cost' of the machinery use was associated with the fertilization activity and 50% with the sanitation practice, which is part of plant protection.

With regard to the environmental impacts associated with the main processes of production of one ton of apple fruit because there were no co-products, no allocation was done in accordance with the ISO standards: 100% of the environmental impacts corre- sponded to one ton of fruit in the analyzed apple orchard. Furthermore, grade specific (i.e., direct consumption; processing) allocation of apples was not conducted in this analysis, as has been undertaken in some past apple LCA research (e.g., Mila i Canals et al., 2006; Sim et al., 2007). Although co-production of grade specific apples occurs, both those intended for direct consumption and those for processing are sent to storage facilities before reaching their final destinations, thereby using inputs involved in the storage process. Separate partitioning of apple grades was also unnecessary due to the fact that an integrated average of apples stored throughout the year was used to determine the amount of materials used per tonne of apples, following the project's objec- tives to understand the environmental impacts of typical com- mercial (conventional and organic) apple production in China, rather than those with superior or inferior economic value. More- over, grade specific data were unavailable for this study.

32: In the present study, the following environmental impacts were considered: non-renewable energy depletion (NED), water deple- tion (WD), land occupation (LO), global warming potential (GWP),
acidification potential (AP), eutrophication potential (EP), aquatic toxicity potential (ATP), human toxicity potential (HTP), and soil toxicity potential (STP).

NED was determined according to Frischknecht et al. (2004). WD and LO were based on the methodology developed by Wang et al. (2007). The GWP over 100 years was calculated according to the CO2 equivalent factors from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC, 2013). EP, AP, and ATP were estimated ac- cording to the EDIP97 method (Hauschild and Wenzel, 1998). HTP was calculated according to the CML01 method (Guinée et al., 2001), and STP was calculated according to Nemecek et al. (2008). In this study, only the residues of heavy metal and pesti- cide had been considered to be the sources of toxicity impacts, residues of heavy metal and pesticide for STP, and residues of pesticide for HTP and ATP. The following toxicity impacts were considered in conventional apple: HTP, STP, and ATP. In the organic apple system, we only analyzed the STP due to none usage of chemical pesticides in the organic apple production.

EP, AP, and ATP were estimated ac- cording to the EDIP97 method (Hauschild and Wenzel, 1998). HTP was calculated according to the CML01 method (Guinée et al., 2001), and STP was calculated according to Nemecek et al. (2008). In this study, only the residues of heavy metal and pesti- cide had been considered to be the sources of toxicity impacts, residues of heavy metal and pesticide for STP, and residues of pesticide for HTP and ATP. The following toxicity impacts were considered in conventional apple: HTP, STP, and ATP. In the organic apple system, we only analyzed the STP due to none usage of chemical pesticides in the organic apple production.
34: Focusing on fertilizer and pesticide, two scenarios were modeled to explore the effects of future and hypothetical changes to the apple production system in order to understand how they impact life cycle burdens. Scenario models were designed by considering possible changes to the base line model that may have an effect on environmental performance. All scenarios were con- structed using data from conventional apple production system and analyzed using impact categories identified in section 2.2.4.

The first scenario ('organic fertilization, OF') assumes that 50% of the currently used N/P/K chemical fertilizers for apples in Shandong and Shaanxi conventional production system (i.e. 836 and 6641 ha-1-year-1), is replaced by an organic fertilizer (i.e. compost) produced from pig and sheep manure. Ge et al. (2017) showed that the current fertilizer application rate in Chinese ap- ple orchard exceeded far more from the experts' recommendation. Some studies also showed that under the conditions of increasing organic fertilizer, the reduction of 25%-55% chemical fertilizer dosage would not affect the yield, and would increase quality. In the second scenario ('no chlorpyrifos, NC'), chlorpyrifos is replaced in conventional production system by low-impact and less toxic pesticides, such as lambda-cyhalothrin, fenvalerate, deltamethrin, chlorantraniliprole, and fipronil. The UK Health and Safety Administration (HSE) have announced that starting on April 1, 2016, the United Kingdom would ban the sale, distribution, and use of chlorpyrifos-containing products. In China, the government has clearly defined the complete ban of chlorpyrifos on vegetables starting on December 31, 2016; however, there is no corresponding provision on fruit trees.

Compared to the conventional system, water depletion was 14.15%-29.95% higher in organic system. The non-renewable energy depletion in SD-CON and SD-ORG
Table 3 Resource use inventories and emissions inventories of a functional unit apple production in the conventional/organic production system. Unit: 1.year-1

systems were 6.94% and 4.11% lower than that in SX-CON and SX- ORG systems, respectively. Compared with the conventional apple production system, the non-renewable energy depletion increased by 10.31% in SD-ORG system and 7.47% in SX-ORG system (Table 3). At the sub-stage level, the energy consumed at different stages varied (Fig. 3). Similar to the results of He et al. (2016) and Liu et al. (2010), we found that the agricultural material input stage con- sumes a large amount of non-renewable energy, accounting for about 81.45% and 83.21% in conventional and organic system, respectively. At the agricultural inputs level, the synthetic fertilizers were identified as the greatest contributors to the total non- renewable energy depletion in conventional system, accounting for 47.11% in SD Province and 50.26% in SX Province, followed paper bags (18.60% in SD Province and 18.70% in SX Province) and pesti- cides (7.20% in SD Province and 8.08% in SX Province). However, in organic system, the production of organic manure was the greatest contributor to energy depletion, accounting for 57.49% in SD Province and 61.24% in SX Province (Fig. 3).

It is noteworthy that the energy consumption of organic system in the off-farm stage was about 30% higher than that of conven- tional system. This was mainly attributable to achieve higher eco- nomic efficiency, whereas organic apple markets were situated in the Beijing, Shanghai, and other far-away big cities, and

transporting more gasoline. On the other hand, a larger proportion of organic apples were stored for longer time for marketed out-of season (Table 1). Unlike the studies of Keyes et al. (2015) and Milà i Canals et al. (2007), energy consumption was minimal in orchard management stage because the very low degree of mech- anization in China, and machineries were exclusively utilized for irrigation and spraying (pesticides), whereas other operations such as fertilization, pruning, thinning, and harvesting were manually performed.

The impact on global warming potential of conventional system was predominated by CO2 and NOx gases (Table 4), whereas organic system mainly involved NOx and N20; these findings were

Fig. 3. Contribution of different stages to energy depletion in the conventional/organic apple production system. AMS: agricultural material input stage, OMS: orchard management stage, OFS: off-farm stage.
Global warming, acidification, and aquatic eutrophication potentials of a functional unit plant production in the conventional/organic apple production system. Unit: kg.t-1-year-1.
The value of CO2-equivalent is according with IPCC (2013), SOx-equivalent and PO4-equivalent are according with Huijbregts et al. (2000) and Deng and Wang (2003).

in agreement with the results of previous studies (Wang et al., 2014; He et al., 2016). Compared with the conventional system, the global warming potential for organic system decreased by 5.31% in SD Province, and 0.63% in SX Province. At the sub-stage level, the agricultural material input stage was the greatest contributor to global warming potential in the conventional system, accounting for 58.52% in SD-CON system and 62.42% in SX-CON system, which can be mainly attributed to emissions during the production of chemical fertilizers, paper bags, and pesticides (Fig. 4). On the other hand, the usage and production of organic mature were the main contributors to global warming potential in the organic system. The off-farm stage of the SD-CON and SX-CON systems respectively contributed 19.04% and 14.24% to the global warming potential, whereas this was 28.01% and 23.46% in organic system in SD Province and SX Province, respectively. The acidification potential of apple production resulted pri- marily from NH3 volatilization in two production regions (Table 4). The acidification potential in organic system was 20.36%, for SD Province, and 17.11% for SX Province, higher than that of conven- tional system. In line with the studies of Hansen et al. (2001) and Mattsson and Wallén (2003), these studies also showed that

producing unit quality organic products had a relatively higher environmental acidification potential. The usage of organic manure was the greatest contributor to the acidification potential, ac- counting for 77.35% in SD-ORG system and 79.89% in SX-ORG sys- tem, whereas the most important contributor was the usage of fertilizers, accounting for 51.95% in SD-CON system and 55.18% in SX-CON system (Fig. 5).

Table 4 showed that the acidification potential of the organic production system was higher than that of conventional system, and the acidification potential in SX Province was higher than that in SD Province. This is because a large amount of organic manure application can cause ammonia volatilization, especially in alkaline soil with higher pH (SX Province has alkaline soil, whereas SD Province has acidic soil). On the other hand, the increase in the utilization of organic manure has resulted in an increase in N (ni- trogen) losses through leaching and runoff (Du et al., 2011).

The aquatic eutrophication potential, which is expressed as kg per PO4-equivalents, represents the contribution to ecosystems in river and lakes. The off-farm stage of the SD-CON and SX-CON systems respectively contributed 19.04% and 14.24% to the global warming potential, whereas this was 28.01% and 23.46% in organic system in SD Province and SX Province, respectively. The acidification potential of apple production resulted pri- marily from NH3 volatilization in two production regions (Table 4). The acidification potential in organic system was 20.36%, for SD Province, and 17.11% for SX Province, higher than that of conven- tional system. In line with the studies of Hansen et al. (2001) and Mattsson and Wallén (2003), these studies also showed that

producing unit quality organic products had a relatively higher environmental acidification potential. The usage of organic manure was the greatest contributor to the acidification potential, ac- counting for 77.35% in SD-ORG system and 79.89% in SX-ORG sys- tem, whereas the most important contributor was the usage of fertilizers, accounting for 51.95% in SD-CON system and 55.18% in SX-CON system (Fig. 5).

Table 4 showed that the acidification potential of the organic production system was higher than that of conventional system, and the acidification potential in SX Province was higher than that in SD Province. This is because a large amount of organic manure application can cause ammonia volatilization, especially in alkaline soil with higher pH (SX Province has alkaline soil, whereas SD Province has acidic soil). On the other hand, the increase in the utilization of organic manure has resulted in an increase in N (ni- trogen) losses through leaching and runoff (Du et al., 2011).

50: The toxicity impact category comprises the effects of pesticides and heavy metals on human, aquaculture, and soil ecosystems. In the present study, all three toxicity types were higher in the con- ventional system than the organic system because it does not involve chemical pesticide consumption. Regardless of conven- tional or organic apple production system, the toxicity impacts in SD Province were lower than that in SX Province.

The impact of conventional system on human, aquaculture, and soil toxicity potentials is predominated by pesticides, and the hu- man toxicity potential was predominated by carbendazim and omethoate, whereas the aquatic and soil eco-toxicity potentials were predominated by chlorpyrifos (Table 5). Although the use of chlorpyrifos was relatively small (<8% of total pesticide use), its degradation in water and soil is very slow, thereby causing very high water and soil toxicity.
Although only trace of low-toxic chemicals chemical pesticides were used in organic apple production, the eco-toxicity risk

continues to increase with the high frequency of application of organic fertilizers. In this study, organic manure tends to release heavy metals and is the only contributor to soil eco-toxicity po- tential in the organic system. Mattsson and Wallén (2003) and Longo et al. (2017) also believe that the presence of large amounts of organic manure in organic production systems will lead to a gradual increase in soil toxicity risk, possibly even higher than that of conventional production systems, which is consistent with the results of this study.

61: <header>4. Options for reducing environmental impacts in apple production systems </header>

Despite some uncertainties (e.g., no estimates on the environ- mental impact of infrastructure construction, orchard machinery production, machines used for sorting and handling during storage) on the calculations in this case, our analysis revealed potential options for reducing environmental impacts in apple production in China.
62: <header>4.1. Environmental optimization of the conventional apple production system </header>

The present findings suggest that pesticides had been named as the main source for human toxicity potential, aquatic eco-toxicity potential, and soil eco-toxicity potential in the conventional apple production system. To reduce the impacts caused by chemical pesticides application, producers could further integrate behav- ioral, biological, and chemical tactics to control pests instead of mainly relying on chemical-based targeted spraying regimes, as well as sharing of spray reduction techniques among orchardists and the usage of low-impact and less toxic pesticides (Simon et al., 2010). Planting disease-resistant cultivars, releasing natural en- emies, and employing new technologies such as drift reducing measures are also encouraged to help reduce chemical pesticide use in apple orchards. In addition, more than 90% of apple orchards in China currently use the compact planting with standard root- stock cultivation mode, and thus thinning or changing tree shapes to optimize the ventilation and light conditions may reduce the occurrence of pests and diseases, thereby reducing the use of chemical pesticides.

In addition, production and the application of synthetic fertil- izers and their related emissions to air and water are the main contributors to global warming potential, aquatic eutrophication potential, and acidifying, as well as to non-renewable energy con- sumption in the conventional apple production system. Therefore, technological adaptations that would reduce the use of synthetic fertilizers should be considered. For instance, encouraging the wider use of integrated soil-crop system management, which manages total N, P, and K fertilizers to match high-yielding crop N, P, and K requirements in time, space, and quantity (Chen et al., 2011), and the selection of fertilizers and manures with less N content and those less prone to subsequent field-level emissions (Brentrup et al., 2001). It is encouraging that the Chinese govern- ment has put forward the goal of 'Zero Growth in Use of Chemical Fertilizers and Pesticides by 2020'. For apples that utilize high amounts of fertilizers and pesticides, a 25% reduction in the application of fertilizer and a 35% reduction in pesticide applica- tions is encouraged by 2020. If it is achieved, then the environ- mental impact of conventional Chinese apple production will be reduced. Furthermore, if the highly toxic chlorpyrifos in the pesti- cide is banned (>50% of water toxicity and soil toxicity is caused by chlorpyrifos), then the environmental impact value of conventional Chinese apple production will be further reduced.
4.2. Environmental optimization of organic apple production system
This study shows that despite a lower productivity, preferring

organic apples versus conventional apples could help to reduce the environmental impacts for most of the examined impact categories. Therefore, the optimal choice for reducing environmental risks could be made by the conversion from the conventional production to the organic production systems, which might ban the use of chemical pesticides and synthetic fertilizers. However, our study also found that the extensive use of organic manure in organic production results in a higher rate of energy depletion, water eutrophication, and soil toxicity. In addition, in modern intensive livestock and poultry farming, organic manure presents high heavy metal content, which may cause soil and water contamination (Yang et al., 2013), especially in long-term application (Wang et al., 2017). Thus, the concernment on 'how' to reduce the environ- mental risks caused by the application of organic manure is a key issue for the organic apple production system. Therefore, in the organic apple production process, optimizing the types and appli- cation amounts of organic manure is of particular importance.

65: <header>4.3. Other options for mitigating environmental impacts </header>

Bagging of fruits in China is extensively used to protect fruits from bird biting and pest infestation, and to increase the quality of the fruits in terms of sugar content and external appearance. However, bagging has also brought a series of negative effects such as increase in labor costs and economic costs, as well as higher incidence of bitter pox and other plant infectious diseases. Our results also show that the production of paper bags will result in relatively greater energy consumption (even greater for organic production) and emissions of a wide range of pollutants. Therefore, researchers should strengthen studies on non-bagging production technologies to reduce environmental emissions.

Currently, in China, especially in economically underdeveloped Shaanxi Province, >80% of fruit growers are using normal cold frame, and the energy consumption of this ordinary cold frame is whereas only is required for controlled atmosphere (CA) frame (Blanke and Burdick, 2005; Milà i Canals et al., 2007). Therefore, increasing the capability of cold frame could reduce energy use and green- house gas emissions in the off-farm stage, especially in organic production system. In addition, consumers should be encouraged to eat seasonal fruit to reduce the refrigeration time of apple.

The transport of agricultural materials (especially organic manure) and the transport of fruits from orchards to retail outlets (especially organic apples) also consume large amounts of energy and emit more pollutants. We recommend that growers try to buy agricultural products from local markets and consumers buy locally produced apples, which will help reduce the environmental impact.

67: The LCA methodology can support the development of studies that aim at reducing energy consumption and environmental im- pacts throughout the supply chain of products and can contribute to the application of sustainable production and consumption strategies.

Comparing LCA data of organic and conventional apple pro- duction in Bohai Bay area and Loess Plateau area in China, the ob- tained results highlighted the environmental advantages of organic production system. These are mainly linked to potential reductions in the use of pesticides and synthetic fertilizers. However, the environmental implications of accompanying trends towards cold storage as well as greater water and land demands within the organic system should be considered. Also, more effort should be made to help organic manure production enterprises and organic

farmers to produce and apply organic manure more efficiently in order to reduce remaining significant impacts in terms of energy depletion, soil eco-toxicity, and eutrophication. Taking these hot- spots into consideration, improvement recommendations were developed with the goal of reducing the life cycle environmental impacts of apple supply chains. Our results also show that, regardless of the conventional pro- duction system or organic system, the environmental impact of the Bohai Bay area is lower than that of Loess Plateau area. The main reason is that the level of apple yield in the Loess Plateau is too low, mainly because the Loess Plateau is in arid area and there is no irrigation condition. Therefore, policy maker need to consider and propose the appropriate yield level and the scientific input rate of fertilizer and pesticides in the Loess Plateau area. Moreover, the systems examined in this study have some of their impacts beyond the farm gate, resulting in environmental burdens that are not accounted for when the LCA stops at the farm gate, especially in organic apples. This finding highlights the importance to take into account life-cycle steps following the cultivation process, especially for food that is not immediately consumed and that needs to be stored. To complete this study, systems boundaries will be further extended to embrace the whole life of apple production, from plant production and plantation to final consumer disposal, in order to estimate the overall impact.

The present LCA study incorporated important aspects (e.g., contribution analysis, sensitivity analysis) into one robust approach that integrated data pertinent to specific site conditions as well as generic data from reliable databases. The results obtained can be used by several end-users (i.e. farmers, agronomists), policy makers and other stakeholders. They can also be applied in the similar climate conditions, in the east Asia and elsewhere.

In line with conclusions drawn by Mila i Canals et al. (2006) and Keyes et al. (2015), we argue that while the scientific evidence provided by LCA studies is essential, of perhaps equal importance is the implementation of improvement recommendations, for miti- gating the impacts of global food systems means that real world changes must be made. As such, effective dissemination of LCA results is paramount, producing and communicating improvement possibilities in ways that are relevant to producers, industry, and government they affect. To do so, presenting results in such ways that relate to socio-economic and political needs is crucial, contextualizing improvements so that benefits can be understood beyond the ecological realm. Although beyond the scope of this article, this is an avenue for future research endeavors.
